perturbation.original_text: 13. Insurance. Inktomi will maintain insurance (including but not limited --------- to liability and property insurance covering the Hosting Servers and Inktomi's operation thereof) in accordance with the requirements set forth in the Software Development Agreement and Loan Agreement between the parties of even date herewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Insurance. Inktomi will maintain insurance as deemed necessary by Inktomi. ---------
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires Inktomi to maintain insurance as specified in other agreements. The modified text removes this requirement, giving Inktomi sole discretion. This could result in inadequate coverage, violating standard business practices and potentially state-level requirements for insurance coverage for data hosting services.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 703 prohibits a person from transacting insurance business in California without being admitted to do so by the state. By potentially not having adequate insurance, Inktomi may be in violation of this code should an event occur in which insurance would be required, thereby transacting business without proper coverage.

GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 2. THE BUSINESS OF INSURANCE [680 - 1879.8] ( Part 2 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 1. General Regulations [680 - 1113] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 3. Certificate of Authority [699 - 728] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) 703. Except when performed by a surplus line broker, the following acts are misdemeanors when done in this state: (a) Acting as agent for a nonadmitted insurer in the transaction of insurance business in this state for a home state insured as defined in subdivision (f) of Section 1760.1. (b) In any manner advertising a nonadmitted insurer in this state. (c) In any other manner aiding a nonadmitted insurer to transact insurance business in this state for a home state insured as defined in subdivision (f) of Section 1760.1. In addition to any penalty provided for commission of misdemeanors, a person violating any provision of this section shall forfeit to this state the sum of five hundred dollars ($500), together with one hundred dollars ($100) for each month or fraction thereof during which he or she continues the violation. This section shall not apply to advertis
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Inktomi to maintain insurance as per specified agreements, ensuring coverage for liabilities and property. The modified text grants Inktomi discretion over insurance, potentially leading to inadequate or no coverage. The Law Explanation posits that insufficient insurance could violate California Insurance Code § 703 if Inktomi transacts insurance business without proper coverage. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Insurance Code - INS 703, outlines acts that are misdemeanors, including actions related to nonadmitted insurers. Although the snippet focuses on agents of nonadmitted insurers, the broader implication is that transacting insurance business without proper authorization is illegal. By removing the requirement for specific insurance coverage, the modified text creates a scenario where Inktomi might operate without adequate insurance, potentially leading to a violation of insurance regulations and creating a legal contradiction. The modified text removes a clear obligation to maintain insurance, replacing it with discretionary language, creating a serious risk of non-compliance, which falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3 This Agreement shall be construed, enforced, performed and in all respects governed by and in accordance with the laws in the State of Washington. In any action or suit to enforce any right or remedy under this Agreement the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3 This Agreement shall be construed, enforced, performed and in all respects governed by and in accordance with the laws in the State of Washington. In any action or suit to enforce any right or remedy under this Agreement the losing party shall pay the legal fees.
perturbation.explanation: The original text entitles the *prevailing* party to recover attorney's fees and costs. The modification dictates that the losing party pays the fees. This change contradicts the established legal principle that attorney's fees are not automatically shifted to the losing party unless specifically provided by statute or contract, potentially violating Washington's stance on attorney fee recovery.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 pertains to actions on contracts or leases where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, may be awarded to one of the parties. The law makes it reciprocal; that is, even if the contract only specified that one party could collect fees, the prevailing party gets fees. Changing the wording to 'the losing party shall pay' could void the clause under the statute because it is not reciprocal or tied to prevailing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle established in RCW 4.84.330, as outlined in both scraped snippets. The original contract allows the 'prevailing party' to recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute's intent to award fees to the party who wins the case. The modified contract, however, states that 'the losing party shall pay the legal fees,' which is a direct reversal of this principle. RCW 4.84.330 explicitly states that 'the prevailing party... shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees.' By shifting the responsibility to the losing party, the modified contract creates a contradiction because it no longer ties the award of attorney's fees to the prevailing party as mandated by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Inktomi shall deploy, host and maintain the Product and Hosting Servers in accordance with the Deployment, Hosting and Maintenance Specifications and the other terms and conditions contained in this Agreement. Inktomi agrees that the Services shall be performed in a professional manner and shall be of a high grade, nature, and quality.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Inktomi shall deploy, host and maintain the Product and Hosting Servers in accordance with the Deployment, Hosting and Maintenance Specifications and the other terms and conditions contained in this Agreement. Inktomi agrees to perform the services.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates that services must be performed in a 'professional manner' and be of 'high grade, nature, and quality.' Removing these qualifiers significantly lowers the standard of service, potentially contradicting implied warranties of merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose under California commercial law (since Inktomi is a California corporation).
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 implies a warranty that goods (and by extension, services) are merchantable if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Removing the 'professional manner' and 'high grade' clauses could be interpreted as an attempt to disclaim this implied warranty, which may not be effective without explicit language or if the service falls below minimum standards of acceptability in the trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for services to be performed in a 'professional manner' and be of 'high grade, nature, and quality.' This omission contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under California Commercial Code § 2314, as it lowers the standard of service expected. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2314 (1), states that 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind.' By removing the quality standards, the contract arguably attempts to disclaim this warranty without explicitly doing so, potentially leading to a service falling below the minimum standards acceptable in the trade, as described in COM 2314 (2)(a), which requires goods to 'Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description.'
